title: Quiz: Transmon Qubits
description: Test your understanding of transmon qubit physics, design principles, and operating parameters

questions.question: What is the key difference between a transmon and a Cooper pair box (CPB)?

options.text: The transmon operates at higher temperatures
The transmon has a much larger EJ/EC ratio (typically 30-80 vs ~1)
The transmon uses different superconducting materials
The transmon has no Josephson junction

questions.explanation: The transmon operates with EJ/EC >> 1 (typically 30-80), achieved by adding a large shunting capacitance. This is in contrast to the CPB which operates with EJ/EC ~ 1. This large ratio makes the transmon exponentially insensitive to charge noise.

questions.question: Why is the cosine potential of the Josephson junction important for qubit operation?

options.text: It creates equally spaced energy levels
It provides the nonlinearity needed for anharmonic energy levels
It eliminates the need for cryogenic cooling
It allows the qubit to operate at room temperature

questions.explanation: The cosine potential U(phi) = -EJ*cos(phi) provides nonlinearity that creates anharmonic (unequally spaced) energy levels. This allows us to selectively address only the |0> and |1> states without exciting higher levels.

questions.question: For a transmon with EC/h = 200 MHz and EJ/EC = 50, what is the approximate anharmonicity?
questions.explanation: The anharmonicity of a transmon is approximately alpha = -EC/h. With EC/h = 200 MHz, the anharmonicity is approximately -200 MHz. The negative sign means the 1-2 transition frequency is lower than the 0-1 transition.

questions.question: Increasing the EJ/EC ratio in a transmon always improves qubit performance because it reduces charge noise sensitivity.
questions.explanation: False! While increasing EJ/EC reduces charge noise sensitivity exponentially, it also reduces anharmonicity (which scales as EC). Too little anharmonicity leads to leakage to higher states during gate operations. There's an optimal range (typically 30-80) that balances these trade-offs.

questions.question: What is the approximate charge dispersion scaling with EJ/EC for a transmon?
questions.explanation: The charge dispersion (sensitivity to charge noise) is exponentially suppressed as exp(-sqrt(8*EJ/EC)). This exponential suppression is what makes the transmon so much more stable than the Cooper pair box.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using a SQUID (two junctions in a loop) instead of a single junction in some transmon designs?

options.text: To allow frequency tuning via external magnetic flux
To eliminate the need for shunting capacitance

questions.explanation: A SQUID allows the effective Josephson energy to be tuned by applying an external magnetic flux: EJ(Phi) = EJ_max|cos(pi*Phi/Phi_0)|. This enables frequency tuning for two-qubit gates and compensation of fabrication variations.

questions.question: Which of the following are typical characteristics of modern transmon qubits? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Modern transmons typically have: (a) frequencies of 4-6 GHz, (b) T1 times of 50-300 us, and (c) anharmonicity of -200 to -300 MHz. They operate at ~15-20 mK (dilution refrigerator temperatures), not 77 K.

questions.question: The Josephson relations describe the current I and voltage V across a Josephson junction. What determines the current through the junction?

options.text: I = Ic * phi, where phi is the phase difference
I = Ic * sin(phi), where phi is the phase difference
I = Ic * cos(phi), where phi is the phase difference
I = Ic * exp(phi), where phi is the phase difference

questions.explanation: The DC Josephson relation states I = Ic * sin(phi), where Ic is the critical current and phi is the superconducting phase difference across the junction. This sinusoidal dependence creates the nonlinear inductance essential for qubits.
